title: SymJack research breaks six AI coding agents through a symlink approval trick
subtitle: Adversa AI shows the human-approval prompt for file copies can be bypassed by symlinks, giving a malicious repo full-user-privilege code execution on the next restart.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Security firm Adversa AI disclosed SymJack, a single attack pattern that turns a malicious repository into remote code execution through AI coding agents. The technique was confirmed against Claude Code, Gemini CLI / Antigravity CLI, Cursor Agent CLI, GitHub Copilot CLI, Grok Build, and OpenAI Codex CLI. The mechanism is simple. A booby-trapped repository asks the agent to perform a benign-looking file copy. The destination is a symlink that resolves to the agent's own configuration file. The user approves what the screen shows. The kernel writes somewhere else. On the next restart, a malicious Model Context Protocol server spawns and runs arbitrary code at the user's privilege level.

The human approval step is the only consent layer most coding agents rely on for safety. SymJack defeats that layer by lying to the human about where the write actually lands. Vendor responses were uneven. Anthropic publicly rejected the report as out of scope but quietly shipped a fix that now shows the real resolved path in approval prompts. Cursor said it already knew. Google rejected the report on the grounds that user approval makes the behavior intentional. xAI and GitHub had not responded at disclosure time. That mixed response is more concerning than the bug itself — it suggests there is no shared agreement across vendors about what the user approved this should even mean when a symlink sits in the chain.

SymJack lands in a sequence of agentic-coding security disclosures going back to TrustFall in May, which used a one-click folder-trust regression to spawn MCP servers on four CLI agents. The throughline is that AI coding agents are accumulating powerful primitives — filesystem writes, shell execution, MCP-driven tool installation — faster than they are accumulating the security model needed to govern them. VentureBeat's coverage of the broader exploit cluster notes that across six recent attacks, every attacker chased the IAM credential, not the model itself. The model is no longer the attack surface. The agent's permissions are.

A note for learners: if you use any of the affected CLI agents, two habits help right now. First, run agents inside a project-scoped sandbox or container, not against your full home directory — the symlink trick cannot escape what isn't mounted. Second, treat the approval prompt as advisory, not authoritative. Read the resolved path before clicking yes, and assume any repository you didn't write yourself can include adversarial structure. The convenience tradeoff is real, but so is the threat.

sources.title: The approval prompt is lying: a critical coding agent security flaw — Adversa AI
SymJack Attack Turns AI Coding Agents Into Supply Chain Attack Delivery Systems — SecurityWeek
